ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Cereal Prawn; mapped to generic prawn dish class as closest defensible anchor. Dish is a Singaporean-Chinese preparation not individually catalogued in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for deep-fried prawn dish with cereal coating
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Cereal Prawn (Singaporean-Chinese preparation). A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Cereal Prawn or equivalent Singaporean-Chinese seafood dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Cereal Prawn is approximately 350 g, comprising 8–10 medium deep-fried prawns coated in fragrant butter-cereal mixture, typically shared among 2–3 diners as part of a Zi Char meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Profile of Popular Zi Char Dishes — Cereal Prawn
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo / HPB Zi Char nutrient profile data. Micronutrients (potassium, magnesium, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked prawns (FDC ID 175180) and oat cereal (FDC ID 173904), weighted to reflect dish composition. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with HPB data and USDA cooked prawn profiles. All values expressed per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — oat-based cereal and mixed seafood dish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cereal Prawn. GI of 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy: instant oat cereal (GI ~55–65, University of Sydney GI Database) forms the primary glycaemic driver of the dish coating. The high protein content from prawns (~15 g/100 g) and substantial fat from butter (~21.7 g/100 g) are well-established moderators of postprandial glucose response, supporting a medium GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 29.1) / 100 ≈ 17.4 for a 350 g serving (carbohydrate_g per serving = 8.3 × 3.5 = 29.1 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (400 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat (8.3 g/100 g) from butter and deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Cereal Prawn to occasional consumption; a 350 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 1,400 mg sodium (over 60% of the 2,000 mg daily limit) and 29 g of fat including 29 g saturated fat, both of which elevate blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~60) driven by refined oat cereal and added sugar in the coating, partially offset by high protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Cereal Prawn in small portions and infrequently; the medium GI cereal coating and added sugar contribute a glycemic load of approximately 17 per restaurant serving, and the high saturated fat content may worsen insulin resistance with regular intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (8.3 g/100 g), cholesterol (150 mg/100 g), trans fat (0.17 g/100 g), and sodium (400 mg/100 g) from deep-frying in butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Cereal Prawn; a single restaurant serving provides approximately 29 g saturated fat and 525 mg cholesterol, substantially exceeding recommended daily limits for saturated fat (<20 g/day) and dietary cholesterol, and the trans fat and sodium content further elevate atherogenic and hypertensive risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory deep-fried preparation and high refined carbohydrate and sugar content; prawns are also high in purines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or gout should limit Cereal Prawn due to its deep-fried, high-fat preparation and the purine content of prawns, which may exacerbate joint inflammation and trigger gout flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cereal Prawn (麦片虾 / 麥片蝦, Màipiàn Xiā) is a quintessentially Singaporean-Chinese dish originating from the Zi Char (煮炒) hawker and restaurant tradition. Large whole prawns are first deep-fried at high heat until their shells become thin, golden, and edible, then tossed in a wok with a fragrant mixture of butter, fresh curry leaves, sliced bird's eye chillies, and toasted Nestum oat cereal that has been enriched with milk powder and sugar. The result is a dish of extraordinary textural contrast: succulent, juicy prawn meat encased in a crispy shell, enveloped in a sweet-savoury, buttery, and intensely aromatic cereal crust. Nutritionally, Cereal Prawn is energy-dense at approximately 283 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (21.7 g/100 g, of which 8.3 g is saturated fat from butter) and a moderate protein content (15 g/100 g) from the prawns. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg/100 g. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~60) due to the oat cereal and added sugar in the coating, though the high protein and fat content moderates the postprandial glucose response. Clinically, it is a high-concern food for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, cholesterol, and trans fat load. It is a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is best enjoyed as an occasional indulgence, shared family-style at lunch or dinner, and is not suitable for low-calorie, low-fat, or low-sodium dietary regimens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cereal prawn?
Is cereal prawn high in sodium?
What is Zi Char cereal prawn?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Zi Char nutrient profile data (primary approved source for Singaporean dishes). Micronutrients estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked prawns and oat cereal, weighted by dish composition — no single approved source provides a complete micronutrient panel for this specific dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database oat cereal data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Cereal Prawn (Singaporean-Chinese preparation) is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite deep-fried dish is not reported in the HPB Zi Char nutrient profile or USDA SR Legacy cooked prawn entries used. trans_fat_g of 0.17 g/100 g is a low but non-zero value consistent with butter-based deep-frying; retained as reported. Halal status marked false as the dish commonly uses butter which may not be certified halal in all preparations; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been removed as the dish contains 8.3 g carbohydrate/100 g from oat cereal and sugar, which is inconsistent with a ketogenic dietary classification. Portions in the input contained duplicate entries and multiple is_default:true flags; the 350 g plate (approx. 8–10 medium prawns) has been selected as the single default serving size as the most representative restaurant portion. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (60 × 29.1) / 100 ≈ 17.4 for a 350 g serving.
